summary: Gruber expresses genuine enthusiasm for Android's potential while tempering it with skepticism about its execution. He clarifies that his loyalty is to great products, not Apple specifically, and would welcome an Android phone that matches the iPhone. However, he identifies Android's fundamental structural weakness: Google doesn't control the hardware, leaving the final product in the hands of carriers and manufacturers with poor track records. He predicts the only path to an iPhone-caliber Android device would be Google making its own phone, which they've said they won't do.
key_points: Gruber's allegiance is to great products, not Apple — he'd welcome Android matching or beating the iPhone
Android's open 'code what you want' philosophy is a compelling contrast to Apple's controlled platform
Apple's key advantage is vertical integration: controlling hardware, software, and user experience end-to-end
Google's reliance on hardware partners and carriers puts the final product in the hands of companies with poor design histories
The only likely path to an iPhone-quality Android phone would be a Google-designed 'gPhone' reference device
money_quotes: My obsession is with wonderful, thoughtful software and gadgetry. I love the iPhone because it's fucking amazing, not because it's from Apple.

If things work out ideally with Android, it's easy to imagine how Android, as an overall platform, could wind up being better than the iPhone, or at least could force Apple to open the iPhone software platform further. But that's an enormously big if.
The big advantage Apple has with the iPhone is that they control the entire product, top to bottom.

Google's dependence on hardware and carrier partners puts the final product out of their control — and into the control of companies whose histories have shown them to be incompetent at design and hostile to users.
My hunch is that the only way we'll see an iPhone-caliber Android phone is if Google does what they've said they're not going to do, which is to design and ship their own reference model 'gPhone'.
I have high hopes for Android, but my expectations are pretty low.
key_insight: Android's structural reliance on hardware partners and carriers — companies historically incompetent at design — is its fundamental weakness against Apple's vertically integrated iPhone.
